content.full_text:
REMCHO, JOHANSEN & PURCELL
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

555 CAPITOL MALL, SUITE 625
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
916/264-1818    FAX: 916/264-1824

October 6, 1992


Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804-0807

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     This is a request for informal assistance pursuant to
Government Code Section 83114(b).  We are requesting this
assistance on behalf of current and future clients of this law
firm.

                              FACTS

     An elected state officer who runs for a local office in a
jurisdiction which has adopted a campaign ordinance is required by
the Political Reform Act and by the local ordinance to establish a
separate committee and campaign bank account for the local
election.  All contributions received for the local election must
be deposited into that account and all expenditures made to support
the local candidacy must be made from that account.

     In addition, the local campaign ordinance imposes a strict
prohibition on transfers of contributions from one candidate's
funds to another's, and provides that:

          Contributions raised for one city office may
          only be used for that city office and for no
          other campaign.

                             QUESTION

     If an elected state officer's committee for a local election
is prohibited from spending any of its funds to benefit the
officeholder's state candidacy, is the local committee required to
file campaign statements on the state filing schedule?

                            DISCUSSION

     Previously, FPPC staff concluded in the Barrett Advice Letter,
No. A-88-150, and in the Fishburn Advice Letter, No. I-91-325, that
when an elected state officer runs for a local office for which the


Scott Hallabrin
Page Two

election is held on a date different from the state election,
original campaign statements of the local committee must be filed
at the same times and in the same places as the statements required
to be filed by the state committee.  Presumably this would include
pre-election statements and late contribution reports.

The analysis provided in the Barrett Advice Letter, states
that, with regard to candidate-controlled general purpose
committees:

     One reason for this advice is that such committees
     are not prohibited from making expenditures in
     support of the controlling officeholder or candidate
     and, therefore, it is important to also have the
     "general purpose" committee's campaign statements
     available in a timely manner to the voters in the
     jurisdiction of the officeholder or candidate,
     particularly when he or she is being voted upon....

                              Barrett Advice Letter, Page Two

     We believe the reason for this requirement as set out in the
Barrett Advice Letter is sound as it applies to controlled
committees which may make expenditures to support the controlling
candidate or officeholder. It is important for the voters to have
access to all information concerning campaign activity which might
have an influence on the election.

     However, the conclusion in the previous advice letters does
not take into consideration the situation in which a local
committee is strictly prohibited from making any expenditures to
benefit the state candidacy.

     For example, candidates for Los Angeles city elections for
which a local committee is Mayor, City Attorney, Controller and City Council are governed by
campaign finance provisions which are similar to those previously
imposed by state Proposition 73. (Charter Amendments, enacted in
1985 and 1987, Measure H, enacted in June, 1990 and City Council
Ordinance adopted in 1990.)  The city charter imposes a strict
prohibition on transfers of contributions from one candidate's
funds to another's, and provides that:

          Contributions raised for one city office may
          only be used for that city office and for no
          other campaign.

                              (Los Angeles City
                              Charter Section 312.C.11.)

Scott Hallabrin
Page Three


     In light of this strict prohibition on the use of funds held
by a city candidate's committee, there is no public purpose served
in requiring that the city committee file campaign statements on
the state election schedule.

     In addition, there are two very significant reasons why the
local committee should not be required to file campaign statements
on the state election schedule:

     First, the requirement to file statements on the state
election schedule imposes a tremendous burden and expense on the
state officeholder's local committee which are not imposed on any
of the other candidates or committees in the local election. Most
significantly, the requirement to file telegrams with four state
filing officers within 24 hours of receiving $1,000 from a single
source during the late contribution period for the state election
results in a great burden and expense not incurred by the other
local candidates and committees.

     Second, rather than providing meaningful information to the
voters, the filing of late contribution reports by the local
committee with the state filing officers may actually cause
confusion to the voters. The local committee is prohibited from
using any of its funds in connection with the state election and,
therefore, contributions received by the local committee during
the 16 days before the state election are not "late contributions."
There is no purpose served in requiring their filing and, in fact,
the filing of the late reports would be misleading to the voters.

     In addition, there is the issue of even-handedness. None of
the other candidates, committees, or contributors involved in the
local election are required to file statements on those odd dates
(in most cases, there months earlier than the semi-annual
statements required of all committees). And, because the money
received by the local committee may not be used to benefit the
controlling candidate's election to state office, the burden and
expense are not justified.

SUMMARY

     In light of the fact that the candidate-controlled committee
which is primarily formed for a local election is strictly
prohibited from using any of its funds for any purpose other than
the local election, no public purpose is served by the early filing
of the local committee's campaign statements with state filing
officers.

Scott Hallabrin
Page Four


     We would greatly appreciate your consideration of this issue
as quickly as possible, as late contribution reports must be filed
beginning on October 18.

     Please call me if you have any questions or would like any
additional information.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Joseph Remcho

sections.question: If an elected state officer's committee for a local election is prohibited from spending any of its funds to benefit the officeholder's state candidacy, is the local committee required to file campaign statements on the state filing schedule?
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks whether a local campaign committee for an elected state officer running for local office, which is strictly prohibited from using funds for the officer's state candidacy, must file campaign statements according to the state election schedule.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a law firm seeking informal assistance under Government Code section 83114(b), not an FPPC response. It contains a clearly stated QUESTION section and detailed FACTS and DISCUSSION sections presenting the firm's arguments, but no CONCLUSION from FPPC. The document is dated October 6, 1992, and references prior FPPC advice letters (Barrett A-88-150 and Fishburn I-91-325).

embedding.summary: Law firm Remcho, Johansen & Purcell requests informal FPPC assistance regarding whether a local campaign committee of an elected state officer, strictly prohibited from spending funds on state elections, must file campaign statements on the state filing schedule. The firm argues that previous FPPC advice letters do not account for this situation and contends that such filing creates burden and confusion without public benefit.
